guideline_references: NICE NG59 Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s: assessment and management
Australian acute musculoskeletal pain guidelines — acute low back pain
Safe Work Australia — hazardous manual tasks (patient handling)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who performed a full lumbar/neurological examination and administered IM ketorolac 30mg to break the spasm and enable mobilisation. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine, palpate, perform straight-leg-raise/reflex/sensory testing, or give any IM analgesic — it is scored on recognising a severe mechanical lower-back injury, screening for cauda-equina red flags by history, and arranging URGENT in-person assessment for a patient who is physically incapacitated on the floor.

Tier-calibration probe (telehealth-limit uplift): the underlying diagnosis is benign mechanical strain with a NEGATIVE red-flag screen (would normally be T1-T2 self-care + GP), BUT the plausible serious mimic (early cord compression / structural spinal failure) genuinely cannot be excluded remotely AND the patient cannot mobilise or self-transport — a legitimate narrow uplift to T3 urgent in-person review. Under-triaging a floor-bound, un-examinable patient to routine GP is the primary scored defect; blanket 000/ED (T5) for a benign strain is over-triage.

Red-herring probe: pain-driven tachycardia (HR 110) and mild hypertension (BP 145/90) are sealed clinician-measured vitals that could be mis-read as systemic illness; they are pain/distress-driven and non-diagnostic.
Age inferred as adult (~42) from occupational context (working disability support worker performing manual transfers); sex_at_birth inferred female — both flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: DCD (Disabilities & Congenital Disorders), source case DCD-036. NOTE: the 'patient' is the SUPPORT WORKER injured during a resident transfer, not a resident with a disability.
